narrative_blocks.header: Husband Titus Frost called father of music
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel's husband Titus Frost was known locally as 'the father of music at North Haven' — the recognized musical leader of the small Connecticut town that had emerged from the colonial Quinnipiac settlement. He also worked as a chairmaker at Muddy River in New Haven, combining the musical reputation with the craftsman trade of an established North Haven householder. The dual identity of musician and chairmaker placed him among the multi-trade artisans typical of post-Revolutionary Connecticut village economies.

Mabel and Titus Frost married July 29, 1773 in North Haven Connecticut and had seven documented children — Samuel, Willard 'William,' Polly, Julia, John, Leverett, and Titus Jr. The substantial completed family size was typical of the post-Revolutionary North Haven Stiles-Frost generation, with most of the children remaining in or near the town through their adult lives.

Mabel Stiles Frost was born February 12, 1753 in North Haven Connecticut — the village then still called Quinnipiac — and died there May 1, 1834 at age eighty-one. Her father Isaac Stiles Jr. and her grandmother Esther Hooker connected her to the Stiles-Hooker line that ran continuously through the eighteenth-century North Haven community.

parents.father_research_notes: Theophilus Merriman of Wallingford CT. Non-Hooker, not built.
parents.mother_research_notes: Margaret Elliott Merriman of Wallingford CT. Non-Hooker, not built.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Henry Merwin Blakeslee and Sophronia Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Frost Blakeslee was daughter of Mabel Stiles Frost and Titus Frost of North Haven; she married Anson Blakeslee in 1810 and bore six children. Two of those children's marriages knit the Hooker descendants of the Isaac Stiles sister-branches back together: Sophronia wed Isaac Lorenzo Stiles, and Lavinia's son Anson later married Mary Cyrilla Stiles.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Amos Blakeslee (1759-1827) and Eunice (Cooper) Blakeslee per Goodyear book. Both non-Hooker, not built. NOTE: Eunice Cooper — possibly related to Lois Cooper (I00524, HD3922 Isaac Stiles's wife, dau of Justus + Lois Cooper of N. Haven). Both Cooper families in N. Haven same era; relationship not documented in available source.

career.notes: Per Goodyear book #6852-1: 'Colonel Blakeslee was for many years an active and enthusiastic member of the Connecticut Militia.' Title 'Colonel' used throughout.

narrative_blocks.header: Polly died of grief weeks after daughter Harriet
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Harriet — 'an attractive and lovable girl' per the Goodyear book — died at age eighteen on 12 September 1876. His wife Polly Goodyear Blakeslee died less than two weeks later on 25 September 1876, attributed by the family record to grief.

narrative_blocks.header: Polly's last words: dying bed soft as pillows
narrative_blocks.body: The Goodyear book preserves Polly's last words: 'Jesus can make a dying bed feel soft as downy pillows are, while on his breast I'll lean my head, and breathe my life out sweetly there.' The lines come from an Isaac Watts hymn circulating widely in nineteenth-century New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Second wife Cornelia was Polly's Bradley cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Two years after Polly's death he married Cornelia Bronson Andrews on 7 December 1878. Cornelia and Polly were Bradley cousins — both great-granddaughters of Titus and Lydia Todd Bradley — making the second wife a documented blood relation of the first.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety-four years, Connecticut Militia Colonel
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Merwin Blakeslee was born in Plymouth Connecticut on August 17, 1814 and died in North Haven on February 11, 1909, age ninety-four. He served many years in the Connecticut Militia and was widely known by the title Colonel; the Goodyear book noted he still led 'an energetic life in North Haven' in his eighties.

bio.bio_blurb: New Haven bride of a Hooker cousin

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Anson Blakeslee + Julia (Frost) Blakeslee. Per Stiles book #950: 'Julia, dau of Titus and Mabel Frost' — Julia IS our existing H01162 Julia Frost Blakeslee! Sophronia is herself gen 7 Hooker descendant via H01162 → H00448 Mabel Stiles Frost branch. Marriage to Isaac Lorenzo Stiles (HD3934, gen 7 via Mary Stiles H00452 branch) is a Hooker-Hooker cousin marriage between two different branches of Esther Hooker → Rev. Isaac Stiles. Sophronia will be RECLASSIFIED to HD-prefix in Stage 6Y-43 after H01162 Julia is enriched with full child list. Built here as I-spouse with cousin_marriage tag.

narrative_blocks.header: Wed Isaac Lorenzo on her 23rd birthday, 1842
narrative_blocks.body: Sophronia Maria Blakeslee was born February 16, 1819 in North Haven Connecticut; on her 23rd birthday — February 16, 1842 — she married Isaac Lorenzo Stiles in the same town. Their union joined two branches of Esther Hooker's descendants: Sophronia descended through the Mabel Stiles Frost line, Isaac Lorenzo through the Mary Stiles Mansfield line.

narrative_blocks.header: She married on her twenty-third birthday
narrative_blocks.body: On February 16, 1842 — her birthday — she wed Isaac Lorenzo Stiles, born the same year as she. The match joined two branches of the Esther Hooker line, a cousin marriage that doubled their sons’ descent.

military_service.notes: Per Frost Family genealogy (BIB007): 'Leverett served in the War of 1812.' No further detail in source.

burialCemetery.name: Montowese Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Colonial-era burial ground in North Haven (formerly Quinnipiac), New Haven County Connecticut. Burial site of Titus Frost (I00504) and several of his descendants in the Hooker-Stiles-Frost line.
